Q1: My study routine was honestly built around listening more than reading. I'd dedicate about 31 hours a week, mostly in the evenings when my flat was quiet. I'd record lectures or find audio versions of materials and play them while doing other things sometimes. I kept a rough schedule but honestly, I learned to be flexible because life throws curveballs and you just have to adapt.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I tried to maintain some consistency, but with 12 online courses, I had to prioritize based on deadlines. Some weeks I'd be intensely focused on one subject, other weeks I'd spread myself across several. I've learned that rigid schedules don't work for me—I need that flexibility to manage my anxiety.
Q3: Usually it was to check on assignments or see if new materials had been posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to see peer responses or check if there was any feedback on my work. Honestly, the anxiety of potentially missing something important was a strong motivator. I'd rather check too often than miss a deadline.
Q4: Yeah, logging in became almost automatic, especially when I had multiple assignments running simultaneously. It happened naturally because I was juggling so many courses and needed to stay on top of things. I'd check in the morning with my coffee, became like a habit. The peer interactions also pulled me back—seeing what others were discussing made me want to stay connected.
Q5: The video content was genuinely helpful because I could listen to it multiple times. I spent a good amount of time on those—around 40 minutes on average per session. For me as an auditory learner, hearing explanations clicked better than just reading text. The peer interactions were also valuable because hearing different perspectives helped solidify concepts in my mind.
Q6: I'll be honest, I rarely touched the discussion forums for posting. I'd read them sometimes but posting felt overwhelming with my anxiety—I'd overthink what to write. Some reading materials I'd skim or download and never fully engage with on the platform. If something felt like busywork rather than genuinely helpful, I'd deprioritize it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to my assignments or exams. If I could listen to it and immediately apply it to something I was working on, that was golden. Also, anything that my peers were engaging with felt more valuable—if others found it useful, I trusted that judgment. I don't have time to waste on things that don't move the needle.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With the workload I was managing, required activities took priority every single time. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping optional stuff, which added to my anxiety. But I've learned to be strategic—I can't do everything, so I focus on what counts toward my grade.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded audio files and listened to them on my phone during my commute or while cooking. I had notebooks full of handwritten notes from lectures I'd listened to repeatedly. I also used YouTube for supplementary explanations on difficult topics. A lot of my actual learning happened away from any screen the LMS could track.
Q10: Many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or listening to downloaded lectures while walking or exercising. I'd also have study sessions with peers in person where we'd discuss course material for hours. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. My best thinking often happened while I was moving around, not sitting at a computer.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once to get the gist, then download the audio or take detailed notes and work from those offline. My phone had folders full of course materials I'd converted to audio format. I found I retained information better when I could revisit it in different contexts—on the bus, at the gym, wherever.
Q12: There were definitely moments when my anxiety spiked and everything felt overwhelming. Usually this happened when multiple deadlines clustered together or when I received feedback that I found confusing. During those times, I'd still push through because that's what I do, but engagement felt more mechanical than genuine. The CBT sessions helped me recognize these patterns.
Q13: Fatigue was probably my biggest enemy. With 31 hours of study weekly plus extracurriculars, I'd sometimes log into the LMS completely exhausted. Competing responsibilities definitely affected my engagement—some days I'd do the bare minimum on the platform because I was dealing with other life stuff. But I always made sure assignments got done, even when I was running on empty.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have videos playing but my mind was elsewhere, worrying about other things. My anxiety would sometimes make it hard to focus even when I was technically 'engaged' with materials. I'd have to rewatch things multiple times because the first viewing didn't really sink in. The system would show I watched something, but it wouldn't show the three times I rewound it.
Q15: I'd sometimes delay starting big assignments because the anxiety of potentially doing them wrong felt paralyzing. Even knowing they were important, I'd procrastinate by doing smaller, less important tasks first. Eventually I'd push through—my resilience kicks in when it really matters—but those initial avoidance periods were real. The CBT techniques helped me recognize this pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were my primary driver, honestly. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due. I'd be much more focused and intentional about what I accessed when time was limited. Without deadlines, I might have explored more freely, but with 12 courses, I had to be strategic about where my energy went.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement, sometimes in unexpected ways. Positive feedback motivated me to engage more with similar activities. But confusing or critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me hesitant to engage further. I'd have to work through those feelings before I could productively return to the material.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to work within, which I actually appreciated. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me plan my 31 hours of study time. But sometimes the structure felt rigid when life demanded flexibility. I performed best when I could adapt the structure to fit my auditory learning style rather than fighting against it.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it suited how I learn. Having peer interaction opportunities also helped, even if I engaged more as a listener than a poster. What made it harder was when materials were text-heavy with no audio alternatives. Also, my low attendance percentage wasn't really about disengagement—it was about learning in ways that worked better for me.
Q20: I think that could reflect genuine exploration and learning that just doesn't translate into submitted work yet. Maybe they're struggling with anxiety about submission like I sometimes do. Or they could be engaging deeply with materials but having trouble synthesizing it into assignments. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes people are searching for understanding.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My attendance was under 30% but I still achieved Merit with strong assignment scores. I was learning extensively offline through audio materials and peer discussions. The LMS was just one tool among many for me. Performance comes from actual understanding, not from how many times you click on a platform.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when and how often someone accesses materials, and patterns around deadlines. But they completely miss the quality of engagement—whether someone was anxiously scrolling or deeply focused. They miss offline study, peer discussions, and the mental work that happens away from screens. They show behavior, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm genuinely wrestling with the material and making it my own. It's when I'm connecting new concepts to things I already know, asking questions even if just in my head. For me, engagement often sounds like something—a lecture playing, a discussion with peers, my own voice explaining concepts back to myself. It's active mental participation, not passive consumption.
Q24: The LMS would never show the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures while doing other things. It wouldn't capture my 29 peer interactions that happened informally outside the platform. It can't measure how my resilience pushed me through anxious moments to complete 96% of assignments. Most importantly, it can't show the internal journey—the anxiety I managed, the growth I experienced, the genuine learning that happened in my own way.
